Implement tag-based access control (LF-TBAC) for classification-driven data governance. 3. Grant column-level permissions to prevent exposure of sensitive columns (PII, financial data). 4. Use row-level security for multi-tenant data isolation in shared tables. 5. Register all S3 data lake locations with Lake Formation for consistent governance. 6. Audit data access via CloudTrail data events for Lake Formation API calls. 7. Integrate Lake Formation permissions with IAM Identity Center for user-to-data access governance.

common_misconfigurations.issue: S3 bucket policies used for data lake access control instead of Lake Formation
common_misconfigurations.impact: Coarse-grained S3 permissions cannot enforce column-level or row-level security

common_misconfigurations.issue: All columns accessible to analytics users without column-level filtering
common_misconfigurations.impact: Users query sensitive columns (SSN, credit card, salary) without data protection controls

common_misconfigurations.issue: LF-TBAC not used; per-table manual permission grants
common_misconfigurations.impact: Data access governance does not scale; new tables require manual permission configuration

common_misconfigurations.issue: Data access events not logged or monitored
common_misconfigurations.impact: Unauthorized data access or excessive queries not detected or investigated

equivalent_in.azure-entra: Microsoft Purview Data Governance + Azure RBAC

vendor_configurations.description: AWS Control Tower provides automated multi-account governance by establishing a well-architected landing zone with pre-configured guardrails. For IAM governance, Control Tower enforces: mandatory guardrails (preventive SCPs and detective Config rules) that cannot be disabled, strongly recommended guardrails for security best practices, and elective guardrails for specific compliance requirements. Control Tower automatically creates organizational units (Security, Sandbox), configures centralized logging, sets up audit accounts, enables CloudTrail and Config, and deploys IAM Identity Center for multi-account SSO. Account Factory provides a self-service mechanism for creating new accounts with standardized baselines. Control Tower addresses the challenge of maintaining consistent IAM governance across a growing AWS Organizations portfolio.
vendor_configurations.best_practices: 1. Use Control Tower for all new AWS Organizations deployments as the governance foundation. 2. Enable all strongly recommended guardrails for IAM security (MFA, access key rotation, root restrictions). 3. Use Account Factory for all new account creation to ensure baseline guardrails are applied. 4. Customize Account Factory with additional IAM baseline (password policy, default roles, logging). 5. Extend guardrails with custom SCPs and Config rules for organization-specific requirements. 6. Monitor Control Tower drift detection for guardrail compliance status. 7. Integrate Control Tower with IAM Identity Center for centralized multi-account SSO.

common_misconfigurations.issue: AWS accounts created outside Control Tower Account Factory
common_misconfigurations.impact: New accounts lack baseline guardrails, logging, and IAM governance configuration

common_misconfigurations.issue: Strongly recommended guardrails not enabled
common_misconfigurations.impact: IAM security best practices not enforced across the organization

common_misconfigurations.issue: Control Tower drift not monitored or remediated
common_misconfigurations.impact: Guardrails modified or disabled in member accounts without detection

common_misconfigurations.issue: Custom guardrails not implemented for organization-specific requirements
common_misconfigurations.impact: Only AWS-provided guardrails active; organization-specific IAM policies not enforced

equivalent_in.azure-entra: Azure Landing Zones + Management Groups + Azure Policy

vendor_configurations.description: Amazon Verified Permissions provides a managed fine-grained authorization service using the Cedar policy language for application-level access control. Unlike IAM (which controls AWS API access), Verified Permissions controls access within your application: who can view which records, which users can perform which business operations, and how data visibility is scoped. Cedar policies define authorization rules using a principal-action-resource model with conditions. Policies are evaluated by the Verified Permissions service via API calls from your application. The service provides a policy store (centralized management of authorization policies), a schema (defining entity types and relationships), and authorization APIs (IsAuthorized for individual decisions, BatchIsAuthorized for bulk checks). Verified Permissions integrates with Cognito for identity context and CloudTrail for authorization decision auditing.
vendor_configurations.best_practices: 1. Use Verified Permissions for application-level authorization instead of embedding authorization logic in code. 2. Define a schema that models your application's entity types and relationships. 3. Write Cedar policies in a separate policy store, not inline in application code. 4. Use BatchIsAuthorized for UI list filtering to determine which resources a user can see. 5. Integrate with Cognito ID tokens for user identity and group membership context. 6. Enable CloudTrail logging for all authorization decisions for audit and debugging. 7. Test Cedar policies using the built-in policy validation and testing tools.

common_misconfigurations.issue: Authorization logic hardcoded in application code instead of using policy service
common_misconfigurations.impact: Authorization rules scattered across codebase; cannot audit, update, or review centrally

common_misconfigurations.issue: Cedar policies too permissive; default-allow instead of default-deny
common_misconfigurations.impact: Users access resources not explicitly permitted by policy

common_misconfigurations.issue: Authorization decisions not logged to CloudTrail
common_misconfigurations.impact: No audit trail for who accessed what within the application

common_misconfigurations.issue: Schema not defined; unstructured authorization without type safety
common_misconfigurations.impact: Policy errors not caught by validation; authorization decisions may be incorrect
